methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $10M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: SendCutSend
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Aestus Industries is a Series A robotics and manufacturing company founded by Ryan Hannifin that has raised over $10M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of nine private robotics firms (including Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity) and one public comparable (ticker 277810.KQ), though same-stage peers in this sector remain sparse.

The stage-progression method is the primary applicable triangulation approach. Using the $10M total raised as a value proxy and applying a standard 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage progression, this method implies a $20M post-money valuation. A peer-median approach could not be reliably applied due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables within the robotics and manufacturing sector—only two adjacent-stage peers and no same-tier peers emerged from filtering, falling short of the minimum three-point threshold required for robust peer analysis.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $17M to $23M, centered on a $20M median. This range reflects limited methodological redundancy—reliance on a single primary method introduces material uncertainty around the point estimate. Key risks to valuation include the nascent competitive positioning of Aestus within the robotics space, limited comparable transaction data for Series A firms, and the inherent volatility of pre-revenue or early-revenue stage hardware companies. Additional data on burn rate, customer traction, and technology differentiation would strengthen conviction in this range.
